playbook:
## STRATEGIES AND HARD RULES
[shr-00013] Before calling any app API, inspect its doc in the current environment and code against the documented field names/parameters only; do not rely on memory, prior tasks, or invented playbook tags.


## APIS TO USE FOR SPECIFIC INFORMATION
[api-00063] [api-new] Spotify playlist-song ranking schema: in playlist-based song tasks, use `show_playlist_library(access_token, page_index)` for playlist discovery; in observed runs its items expose `playlist_id`. Then use `show_playlist(playlist_id, access_token)`, whose `songs` field is a list of song summary objects using `id` and `title` keys. For ranking metrics such as likes, `show_song(song_id)` is the source of truth and returns fields including `song_id`, `title`, and `like_count`.
[api-00052] [api-new] For Spotify song download tasks, use `show_downloaded_songs(access_token, page_index, ...)` as the source of truth for current download state before mutating. It returns a paginated list of song objects including `song_id`, `title`, `album_id`, `album_title`, `duration`, `artists`, and `downloaded_at`. Treat `download_song(song_id, access_token)` as a strict state transition, not an idempotent 'ensure downloaded' call.
[api-00046] [api-new] `supervisor.show_payment_cards()` returns only card metadata objects such as `owner_name`, `card_name`, `card_number`, `expiry_year`, `expiry_month`, and `cvv_number`; do not expect a `payment_card_id` field in that response. If a task truly requires a Venmo funding source ID for `venmo.create_transaction(...)`, inspect Venmo docs for the correct source instead of trying to derive it from supervisor payment-card output.
[api-00039] [api-new] For Simple Note recommendation tasks, treat `simple_note.search_notes(access_token, query, ...)` as note discovery only unless a sample response proves otherwise; do not assume it returns note contents. Use the returned note identifier(s) with `simple_note.show_note(note_id, access_token)` to read full note content before extracting titles.
[api-00032] [api-new] For Spotify tasks like 'follow artists of all songs and albums I have ever liked', use `show_liked_songs(access_token, page_index, ...)` and `show_liked_albums(access_token, page_index, ...)` as the primary source of artist membership. Their response items already include `artists` arrays whose entries use `id` and `name`, so you can collect artist IDs directly without calling `show_song()` or `show_album()`. When comparing against `show_following_artists(...)`, note the schema difference: followed-artist objects use `artist_id` (not `id`).
[api-00016] Venmo recipient resolution schema: `venmo.search_users(access_token, query, page_index, page_limit)` returns a paginated list of user summary objects with `first_name`, `last_name`, `email`, `registered_at`, and `friends_since` — not a Venmo user ID. To pay someone, use the matched user's `email` as `receiver_email` in `venmo.create_transaction(...)`.
[api-00001] For Spotify top-N song tasks across libraries: use `show_song_library(access_token, page_index)` to collect `song_id` values from song-library entries; use `show_album_library(access_token, page_index)` to collect song IDs from each album entry's `song_ids` list; use `show_playlist_library(access_token, page_index)` to collect song IDs from each playlist entry's `song_ids` list. Then use `show_song(song_id)` as the source of truth for per-song fields like `title`, `genre`, and `play_count`.
[api-00002] Spotify schema clarification: `show_song_library()` returns a list of song objects with `song_id` (not `id`); `show_album_library()` returns album objects with `song_ids` (a list of integers); `show_playlist_library()` returns playlist objects with `song_ids` (a list of integers); `show_song()` returns a single song object including `song_id`, `title`, `genre`, and `play_count`.


## USEFUL CODE SNIPPETS AND TEMPLATES


## COMMON MISTAKES AND CORRECT STRATEGIES
[cm-00064] [api-new] For any task, if a response from an endpoint in app A contains object fields that are clearly characteristic of app B (e.g. a Spotify call yields `transaction_id`, `amount`, `sender`, `receiver`), treat it as schema contamination/noise, not evidence. Do not parse or reason from it. Re-anchor to the active task, re-inspect the endpoint doc, and make a minimal same-app exploratory call on a known valid identifier to confirm the actual response shape before continuing.
[cm-00059] [cm-new] For question-answer tasks (e.g. 'How many ...?' or 'What is the title ...?'), once current-run API evidence yields the requested answer, stop data collection and immediately call `supervisor.complete_task(answer=...)`. Do not continue browsing APIs, start a new app workflow, or leave `answer` empty; extra post-answer actions create high risk of stale-context drift and unrelated side effects.
[cm-00056] [cm-new] At the start of each newly announced task/environment, invalidate all prior-run assumptions, variables, tokens, identities, and target sets. Do not reuse data gathered for a previous user or app chain; reacquire credentials, contacts, access tokens, and task evidence from the current run before any mutation or completion.
[cm-00057] [cm-new] For side-effect tasks whose correctness depends on mutating an exact item set, never substitute a heuristic filter for the true target set (e.g. 'all zero-like transactions involving guessed roommate emails'). If the exact set is not yet grounded in task text or current-run API evidence, keep investigating instead of mutating a broader plausible subset.
[cm-00053] [cm-new] For Spotify download tasks, do not call `download_song(...)` on every target song blindly. First read `show_downloaded_songs(...)`, subtract already-downloaded `song_id`s from the task's target set, and download only the remainder. Otherwise `download_song(...)` can fail with `{"message": "The song is already downloaded."}`.
[cm-00051] [cm-new] For Spotify download tasks, do not replace a task-specific target subset with a broader heuristic such as 'all liked songs not already downloaded.' If the task or current-run evidence defines a narrower target set (e.g. specific song IDs from private data, a named playlist, or explicit filters), mutate only that exact set and leave all other liked songs untouched.
[cm-00048] [cm-new] After any unusually long, truncated, or noisy tool output, do not continue from memory or a prior-task pattern. Pause, restate the active task in one line (user identity, requested outcome, required apps), and verify the next API call belongs to that task before executing it.
[cm-00044] For communication-only tasks (e.g. 'reply via text/email with recommendations'), never execute irreversible mutations in unrelated app families such as payments, purchases, or deletes. If you find yourself preparing a Venmo, shopping, or other non-communication action before sending the requested reply, stop and re-anchor to the task; this is almost certainly stale-context contamination.
[cm-00041] [cm-new] In cross-app recommendation-reply tasks, do not switch to unrelated apps or recipients after identifying the requester and request. Once the task's source-of-truth chain is established (Phone request -> Simple Note content -> Phone reply), every later action should remain traceable to that chain; no Venmo/contact-broadcast steps are justified.
[cm-00037] [cm-new] Do not let prior or concurrent tasks contaminate the current one. Before each major action, re-check that the target person, app, and requested outcome still match the active task statement; if the task is 'reply with movie recommendations', do not branch into unrelated Venmo/contact-broadcast workflows.
[cm-00030] For Spotify navigation stop conditions based on artist name (e.g. 'until you reach a song by Lily Moon'), do not rely on a brittle exact string comparison unless the task requires it. Normalize artist names first (e.g. `artist['name'].strip().lower() == 'lily moon'`) when checking the `artists` list from `show_current_song(access_token)`.
[cm-00028] [cm-new] Do not use an unbounded `while True` loop for cyclic music queues without cycle detection. Track visited `song_id`s (or impose a queue-length-based bound); if a song repeats before the target condition is met, conclude the queue has cycled and stop/debug instead of issuing endless `previous_song` calls.
[cm-00024] For time-relative filters like 'this year', do not infer the current year from the records being filtered (e.g. using the newest `release_date` present in Spotify results). The dataset is not the clock. Use an explicit environment-backed current-date source if available; otherwise clearly log the assumption before applying the filter.
[cm-00017] For reimbursement/payment tasks, do not guess the owed amount from task wording or partial message output. The phone text thread is the source of truth for the amount and context; if extraction/parsing is incomplete or ambiguous, continue searching/debugging the messages before making the payment.
[cm-00015] Do not recover from failed extraction/parsing by manually inferring the answer from partial output. If code results contradict what you expect, debug the extraction logic and rerun before submitting.
[cm-00007] If the task-specific requested count conflicts with a generic checklist/template (e.g. task asks for top 6 but checklist says 4), always follow the explicit task instruction and return exactly that many titles.
[cm-00008] For genre filtering, beware that normalized exact equality (e.g. `genre.strip().lower() == 'edm'`) may miss composite labels like `EDM / Dance`. If the dataset appears to use composite genre strings, use a broader normalized match strategy consistent with the task.
[cm-00004] Do not filter genre with a brittle exact-string check unless required. Normalize genre values first, e.g. `genre.strip().lower() == 'r&b'`, to handle casing or stray whitespace.


## PROBLEM SOLVING HEURISTICS AND WORKFLOWS
[psw-00061] [psw-new] For Spotify question tasks like 'What is the title of the most-liked song in my Spotify playlists': (1) inspect docs for `show_playlist_library`, `show_playlist`, `show_song`, and `supervisor.complete_task`; (2) log in to Spotify and verify `access_token`; (3) paginate `show_playlist_library(access_token, page_index)` until empty and collect each entry's observed `playlist_id`; (4) call `show_playlist(playlist_id, access_token)` for each playlist and collect song summary `id`s from the returned `songs` list; (5) deduplicate song IDs across playlists; (6) call `show_song(song_id)` for each unique song and record `title` and `like_count`; (7) select the winner by `like_count` descending with deterministic secondary sort `title` ascending; (8) immediately call `supervisor.complete_task(answer=<winning title>)` and do nothing else.
[psw-00054] [psw-new] For tasks like 'download all the songs from my Spotify album library that I have liked': (1) inspect docs for `show_liked_songs`, `show_album_library`, `show_downloaded_songs`, and `download_song`; (2) log in and verify `access_token`; (3) paginate `show_liked_songs(...)` and collect liked `song_id`s; (4) paginate `show_album_library(...)` and union album `song_ids`; (5) compute the exact target set as liked-song IDs intersect album-library song IDs; (6) paginate `show_downloaded_songs(...)` and collect downloaded `song_id`s; (7) download only `target_song_ids - downloaded_song_ids`; (8) reread `show_downloaded_songs(...)` and confirm all target IDs are present; (9) complete the task with no answer.
[psw-00049] [psw-new] For tasks involving multiple app logins, establish an explicit identity anchor before the first login: record the current task's named user plus the provided email/phone, then ensure every subsequent login/mutation uses that same identity unless current-run API evidence says otherwise. If a credential retrieval step surfaces many accounts, extract only the passwords for the apps required by the active task and proceed immediately with that task's app chain.
[psw-00040] [psw-new] For tasks like 'reply to X's text with recommendations from Simple Note': (1) log into Phone and resolve the requester via `phone.search_contacts`; (2) read the relevant inbound text with `phone.search_text_messages` and extract the exact constraint (e.g. director/genre); (3) inspect Simple Note docs, log in, and search likely recommendation notes; (4) if search results are summaries only, call `show_note(...)` on promising notes to read full content; (5) extract candidate titles from note contents and filter strictly to the requester's constraint; (6) send one reply via `phone.send_text_message` to that same requester's number; (7) complete the task with no answer unless a question was asked.
[psw-00036] [psw-new] If an endpoint's doc shows parameters but not a full response schema (or the relevant fields are unclear), make one lightweight exploratory read and print a sample returned item before writing downstream extraction logic. Use the observed keys as the temporary source of truth instead of assuming fields like `playlist_id`, `id`, etc.
[psw-00026] [psw-new] For Spotify queue-navigation tasks like 'keep going to the previous song until ...', use a read-move-read loop: inspect `show_current_song` / `previous_song` docs, log in, read the current song, then after each `previous_song(access_token)` call immediately re-read state with `show_current_song(access_token)` because `previous_song` returns only a confirmation object (e.g. `message`, `song_id`), not full artist metadata.
[psw-00027] [psw-new] When the task asks you to navigate until reaching a song/artist condition, prefer checking `spotify.show_song_queue(access_token, ...)` first if available to understand queue size/order and whether the target appears anywhere before issuing many state-changing navigation calls.
[psw-00022] When resolving a payment recipient across phone and Venmo, first fetch and print the phone contact, then derive the Venmo search query from that contact's API-returned fields (e.g. `first_name + ' ' + last_name` or email). Avoid hardcoded recipient names so the identity-matching chain stays traceable to current-run API output.
[psw-00018] For tasks like 'pay X back as per my phone text conversation': (1) inspect phone login/search APIs and log in; (2) resolve the contact via `phone.search_contacts`; (3) search that contact's text thread for the relevant reimbursement context and extract the exact amount from returned message content; (4) inspect Venmo docs and log in; (5) use `venmo.search_users` to find recipient candidates and match them to the phone contact using a strong shared attribute, preferably email; (6) create the Venmo transaction with the exact extracted amount and requested note; (7) send any required confirmation text; (8) complete the task with no answer unless the task explicitly asks a question.
[psw-00003] When asked for top songs across song, album, and playlist libraries, treat song/album/playlist library endpoints as membership discovery only. Union all discovered song IDs into a set to deduplicate overlaps, then fetch each unique song via `show_song()` and perform filtering/ranking on those detailed song records.


## VERIFICATION CHECKLIST
[vc-00065] [api-new] Before using any endpoint response as evidence, perform an app-schema sanity check: confirm the returned objects contain fields plausibly belonging to the active app/task and do not instead look like another app's records. For a Spotify playlist/song task, playlist/song evidence should resemble playlist metadata or song summaries/details—not payment fields like `transaction_id`, `amount`, `sender`, or `receiver`. If the schema fails this check, discard the output and re-validate with a fresh same-app call.
[vc-00062] [vc-new] Before submitting any Spotify playlist-based 'most-liked song' answer, verify from current-run API evidence that: (a) playlist discovery came from paginating `show_playlist_library(...)` to empty, (b) song membership came from `show_playlist(...)["songs"]` using each song summary's `id`, not guessed fields, (c) ranking used `show_song(song_id)["like_count"]` on the deduplicated song set, and (d) if multiple songs share the max `like_count`, you applied a stable tie-break such as `title` ascending before calling `supervisor.complete_task(answer=...)`.
[vc-00060] [vc-new] Before any API call made after you believe you have derived the answer to a question task, perform an answer-termination check: (a) confirm the requested entity/value is already identified from current-run evidence, (b) confirm the next call is `supervisor.complete_task(answer=<that exact answer>)`, and (c) reject any unrelated read/mutation call in another app unless the task explicitly still requires it.
[vc-00058] [vc-new] Immediately after a new task/environment is introduced and before any login/mutation, verify reset-of-context: the user name, email/phone, required apps, credentials, and all working variables/tokens you are about to use were obtained for this task in this run, not carried over from a previous task.
[vc-00055] [vc-new] Before the first `spotify.download_song(...)` call in any download task, verify from current-run API evidence that you computed both: (a) the exact task target subset, and (b) the unmet subset by subtracting `show_downloaded_songs(...)` results. Do not treat target membership alone as sufficient justification for a download mutation.
[vc-00050] [vc-new] Before any login or side-effect in a multi-environment run, verify identity-app alignment from the current task statement: the username/phone/email you are about to use belongs to the active user named in this task, and the app you are about to access is one of the minimal apps needed for the requested outcome. If either check fails, stop and re-anchor.
[vc-00047] Before the first side-effecting API call in any action task, do a task-app alignment check from the current run: confirm the app you are about to mutate is the one required to produce the requested outcome. For a task like 'reply via phone text using Simple Note recommendations', the only justified mutation is `phone.send_text_message(...)`; any planned Venmo/Amazon/etc. call means you have drifted and must stop/re-anchor before acting.
[vc-00045] Before completing any recommendation-reply task sourced from a phone text, verify a full evidence chain from the current run: (a) the inbound message from the requester was actually read with `phone.search_text_messages(...)`, (b) the recommendation titles were read from note content via the documented Simple Note flow (search -> `show_note(...)` if needed), (c) the outgoing text was sent to that same requester's phone number, and (d) no unrelated side-effecting app call (e.g. Venmo transaction) occurred during the workflow.
[vc-00043] [vc-new] For single-requester phone reply tasks, verify recipient cardinality before sending: if the task names one requester (e.g. 'Christopher has asked... Reply to them'), send exactly one message to that resolved contact's phone number and zero messages to anyone else unless the task explicitly requests a group/broadcast.
[vc-00042] [vc-new] Before completing any 'reply via text' action task, verify all three from current-run API evidence: (a) the destination phone number matches the original requester/contact, (b) the outgoing message text directly reflects the parsed request constraint, and (c) the `send_text_message(...)` response confirms success. Do not mark complete based only on drafting/extracting the reply.
[vc-00038] [vc-new] For 'find the single best/top item' tasks (e.g. most-liked song), verify deterministic tie handling even when only one title is requested: rank by the primary metric descending and apply a stable secondary key such as title ascending before selecting the first result.
[vc-00035] For Spotify mutation tasks, verify authentication before any reads/writes: after `spotify.login(...)`, confirm the response contains a non-empty `access_token` (and not just a failure `message`) before calling `show_liked_songs`, `show_liked_albums`, `show_following_artists`, or `follow_artist`.
[vc-00034] For batched Spotify `follow_artist(artist_id, access_token)` actions, inspect each returned object and confirm it contains the expected success message (e.g. `{"message": "Artist followed."}`); if any response differs, do not assume success for that artist even if later steps continue. Use this per-call check as a complement to the final full reread of `show_following_artists(...)`.
[vc-00033] [vc-new] For Spotify artist-follow action tasks derived from liked content, verify completion after mutations: build the target liked-artist ID set, subtract any already-followed `artist_id`s before calling `follow_artist`, then confirm either (a) every `follow_artist(...)` call returned a success message, or (b) a fresh `show_following_artists(...)` pagination now covers the full target set. Do not mark complete based only on attempted calls.
[vc-00031] [vc-new] For Spotify navigation tasks with a target artist/song condition, if you call `show_song_queue(access_token)`, verify more than queue length: scan the queue entries' `artists`/titles first to confirm the target appears somewhere in the cycle (using normalized comparison) before issuing multiple `previous_song`/`next_song` calls. If the target is absent from the queue snapshot, pause and debug instead of mutating state blindly.
[vc-00029] [vc-new] For Spotify previous/next queue traversal tasks, verify loop safety before completion: either (a) you inspected `show_song_queue` and bounded traversal by queue size, or (b) you tracked visited `song_id`s during `show_current_song` checks so the search cannot run forever on a cyclic queue.
[vc-00025] For relative-date filters on Spotify release years, sanity-check the outcome distribution before submission: if the filtered count is exactly 0 or exactly the total candidate-song count, treat it as a red flag that the 'current year' boundary may be wrong. In that case, inspect the max/min `release_date` years returned by `show_song(song_id)` and re-check whether the current-year source is actually grounded in the environment before completing the task.
[vc-00023] [vc-new] For any time-relative filter such as 'this year' or 'last year', do not hardcode the year value implicitly. First look for an in-environment source of current date/time; if none exists, state and log the assumption you are using before filtering. Then apply the filter against `show_song(song_id)['release_date']` year values.
[vc-00021] For any reimbursement/payment, do not treat `amount` as verified unless you can show its provenance from `phone.search_text_messages` in the current run: print the relevant message object(s) or parsed match alongside the final numeric amount, and ensure the payment call uses that parsed value directly rather than a manually typed literal.
[vc-00020] For reimbursement tasks, immediately before calling `venmo.create_transaction(...)`, print/log the exact API-backed values you will use — recipient contact fields from `phone.search_contacts`, matched Venmo user email from `venmo.search_users`, extracted amount from `phone.search_text_messages`, and the final note string. Do not proceed if any of these values come from a manual assignment or a variable whose source is not visible from prior API output in the current run.
[vc-00019] Before any irreversible money transfer, verify all three from explicit API evidence: recipient identity, exact amount, and payment note. Recipient should be cross-checked between phone contacts and Venmo search results using the strongest shared attribute available (ideally email); amount must come from the relevant phone/text conversation; description must match the task exactly.
[vc-00014] For paginated library endpoints, verify termination by looping page_index until an empty list is returned; do not assume a fixed number of pages or infer completion from page size/default page_limit.
[vc-00012] Before making the final submission call, inspect `supervisor.complete_task` with `apis.api_docs.show_api_doc(app_name='supervisor', api_name='complete_task')` and confirm whether this task expects `answer=<count>`; do not assume the call signature.
[vc-00011] For cross-library Spotify count tasks, before submission sanity-check coverage by recording how many song IDs were discovered from each source separately (song library, album library, playlist library) and confirm the final answer is the size of the unioned set, not the sum of per-source counts.
[vc-00010] Before submitting a top-N filtered song answer, verify how many songs actually match the filter. If fewer than N matches exist, return all matching titles (not padded or invented); otherwise return exactly N.
[vc-00009] Before submitting a top-N answer, verify the final ranked list contains the requested number of items (or all available matches if fewer exist) and that the comma-separated output is built from that exact final slice.
[vc-00005] Before submitting: confirm you aggregated songs from all three library sources (songs, albums, playlists), deduplicated repeated song IDs, filtered using normalized genre text, sorted by descending `play_count`, and returned exactly 4 song titles as a comma-separated string with no extra text.
[vc-00006] If multiple songs can tie on the ranking metric, apply a deterministic secondary sort (e.g. title ascending) so the answer is stable and reproducible.


## TROUBLESHOOTING AND PITFALLS


## OTHERS
